name: Templars' Path Loop
name_original: Monasterio de Santa María de Veruela - Ambel — circular desde Talamantes
description: Moderate 24.6-mile road cycling route from Talamantes to the Monastery of Santa María de Veruela, gaining 1948 feet.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Talamantes traverse the varied terrain of the Moncayo Natural Park, situated at the foot of the Peñas de Herrera. The region features well-maintained roads with generally light traffic, offering a compelling blend of challenging ascents and scenic beauty. Cyclists can expect routes with steady gradients, particularly on the Moncayo climb, which is a notable feature of the area. The landscape includes rocky outcrops, ravines, and the diverse ecosystems of the Moncayo Massif. Best road cycling routes around Talamantes The most popular road cycling route is Moncayo Climbs Loop via Tarazona and Veruela, a 52.6 miles (84.7 km) trail that takes 4 hours 41 minutes to complete. This difficult route includes significant elevation gain across the Moncayo Massif. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Aranda Comarca Loop, a difficult 50.5 miles (81.2 km) path. This route offers extensive views of the Aragonese countryside and requires a high level of fitness. Local road cyclists also love the Templars' Path Loop, a 24.6 miles (39.6 km) trail leading through historical landscapes, often completed in about 2 hours 5 minutes. Road cycling around Talamantes is defined by the challenging ascents of the Moncayo Massif, the dramatic Peñas de Herrera, and well-maintained roads. The network offers options for both moderate and difficult ability levels. The routes in Talamantes are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 1 review. More than 100 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Talamantes's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Talamantes?
A: There are 9 road cycling routes available around Talamantes on komoot, offering a range of experiences from moderate to difficult.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes in Talamantes?
A: Road cycling routes in Talamantes traverse varied terrain, primarily within the Moncayo Natural Park and at the foot of the Peñas de Herrera. You can expect well-maintained roads with generally light traffic, featuring challenging ascents, particularly on the Moncayo climb, which often has steady gradients between 4% and 5%. The landscape includes rocky outcrops, ravines, and diverse ecosystems.

Q: Are there any easy road cycling routes suitable for beginners in Talamantes?
A: While there are no routes specifically rated as 'easy' on komoot, there are 6 routes rated as 'moderate'. These routes, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e992909482/templars-path-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Templars' Path Loop&lt;/a&gt;, offer a good balance of challenge and accessibility for those with a reasonable fitness level, often covering around 40-50 km with manageable elevation gains.

Q: What are some of the more challenging road cycling routes in the Talamantes area?
A: For experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e988544215/moncayo-climbs-loop-via-tarazona-and-veruela" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Moncayo Climbs Loop via Tarazona and Veruela&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e989952691/aranda-comarca-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Aranda Comarca Loop&lt;/a&gt; are rated as difficult. These routes feature significant elevation gains and cover distances of over 80 km, providing a demanding yet rewarding experience across the Moncayo Massif and the Aragonese countryside.

Q: What natural features or viewpoints can I see along the road cycling routes?
A: The routes offer stunning natural features and viewpoints. You can experience the majestic Moncayo Massif, the highest peak in the Iberian System, and the dramatic Peñas de Herrera. Specific viewpoints include &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5291398" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cabezo de la Mata&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5304172" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Moncayo&lt;/a&gt;. The area also features diverse landscapes like beech and oak forests, and the confluence of the Valdeherrera and Valdetreviño ravines.

Q: Are there any historical landmarks or attractions to explore near the cycling routes?
A: Yes, Talamantes and its surroundings are rich in history. You can find the remains of a 12th-century castle, the Romanesque Hermitage of San Miguel, and the Church of San Pedro Apóstol with its Gothic mural paintings. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e992909482/templars-path-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Templars' Path Loop&lt;/a&gt; specifically leads through historical landscapes. Additionally, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5316074" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monastery of Santa María de Veruela&lt;/a&gt; is a notable attraction accessible on some routes.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Talamantes?
A: The region around Talamantes is generally favorable for road cycling, with well-maintained roads. While specific seasonal recommendations depend on personal preference for temperature and conditions, spring and autumn typically offer pleasant weather for cycling, avoiding the peak heat of summer and potential colder conditions in winter. The Moncayo climb is a popular route that can be enjoyed across various seasons.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes available in Talamantes?
A: Yes, many of the road cycling routes around Talamantes are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. Examples include the popular &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e988544215/moncayo-climbs-loop-via-tarazona-and-veruela" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Moncayo Climbs Loop via Tarazona and Veruela&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e989952691/aranda-comarca-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Aranda Comarca Loop&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e992909482/templars-path-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Templars' Path Loop&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about cycling in Talamantes?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 1 review. Cyclists often praise the compelling blend of challenging ascents and scenic beauty, particularly the well-maintained roads and generally light traffic that make for an enjoyable experience across the varied terrain of the Moncayo Natural Park.

Q: Are there any routes that feature the Moncayo climb?
A: Yes, the Moncayo climb is a significant feature of the region and is included in several routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e988544215/moncayo-climbs-loop-via-tarazona-and-veruela" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Moncayo Climbs Loop via Tarazona and Veruela&lt;/a&gt; is a prime example, offering a challenging ascent that has even been featured in the Vuelta a España. The climb is known for its long and steady gradients, typically between 4% and 5%.

Q: What is the typical length and duration for road cycling routes in Talamantes?
A: Route lengths vary significantly, from moderate options like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e992909482/templars-path-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Templars' Path Loop&lt;/a&gt; at around 39.6 km (2 hours 5 minutes) to more demanding routes such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e988544215/moncayo-climbs-loop-via-tarazona-and-veruela" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Moncayo Climbs Loop via Tarazona and Veruela&lt;/a&gt;, which spans 84.7 km and takes approximately 4 hours 41 minutes to complete.
tips: We are in the town of Talamantes within the Moncayo natural park, from where we can see the remaining walls of the Talamantes Castle located on top of the hill. Also known as the Silla Mount Fortress

We are in the monastery of Santa María de Veruela, a magical place in the province of Zaragoza. The monastery combines Romanesque, Gothic, Renaissance and Baroque architecture and is surrounded by a wall. We can also visit the cloister or the exhibition of the Bécquer brothers since they worked and stayed here. Admission is free for children up to 11 years old (€2 for adults) but it is totally worth it if we are interested in the whole story.
Also called Palacio de los Hospitalarios, it is a fortress located in the municipality of Ambel. On December 4, 2001, it was declared an Asset of Cultural Interest.

faqs.question: How long does it take to complete this cycling route?
faqs.answer: This circular racebike route typically takes around 2 hours and 5 minutes to complete. However, your actual time may vary depending on your fitness level, speed, and how long you spend at the various points of interest.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this route is generally considered a 'Hard bike ride' and demands 'Very good fitness'. With an elevation gain of nearly 600 meters over approximately 40 kilometers, it's best suited for experienced cyclists looking for a challenge.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The terrain is predominantly paved, making it suitable for racebikes. However, be aware that some segments might be unpaved and could present difficulties. You'll experience diverse landscapes within the Moncayo natural park.

faqs.question: What can I see along the route?
faqs.answer: This route offers a rich blend of natural beauty and historical landmarks. Key highlights include the historic Monastery of Santa María de Veruela, the Castle of Talamantes, and the Palace of the Order of Saint John of Jerusalem. You'll also enjoy views of the Moncayo mountain range and pass through areas with significant cultural heritage.

faqs.question: Where can I park for this route?
faqs.answer: The route is circular from Talamantes, suggesting Talamantes as a convenient starting and parking point. You can also look for parking options near the Monastery of Santa María de Veruela, which is a major highlight along the way.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The Moncayo region can experience varied weather. For cycling, spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant temperatures and conditions, avoiding the extreme heat of summer or potential snow/ice in winter. Always check the local weather forecast before you go.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the route passes through natural areas, it is primarily a road cycling route. Dogs are generally allowed in the Parque Natural del Moncayo, but it's always best to keep them on a leash, especially near roads or in populated areas. Consider if the distance and difficulty are suitable for your dog if they are accompanying you.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay an entrance fee for this route or the Parque Natural del Moncayo?
faqs.answer: Generally, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this route or to access the Parque Natural del Moncayo for recreational activities like cycling. However, always respect local regulations and signage, especially concerning protected areas or private property.

faqs.question: Are there public transport options to the trailhead in Talamantes?
faqs.answer: Public transport options to smaller towns like Talamantes in rural Aragon can be limited. It's advisable to check local bus schedules (e.g., from Zaragoza or larger nearby towns) in advance, as services might be infrequent, especially on weekends or holidays. Driving is often the most convenient way to reach the starting point.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route briefly intersects with several other paths. You'll find sections that overlap with the Camino de la Vera Cruz (El camino de los Templarios), [CIMA ZA05] Moncayo * Monasterio de Veruela, and various segments of the Paseo de Veruela.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or drink along the route?
faqs.answer: The route passes through or near towns like Talamantes and Ambel, where you may find local cafes or restaurants. The area around the Monastery of Santa María de Veruela might also have facilities. However, it's always recommended to carry sufficient water and snacks, especially on a longer ride like this, as services might not be consistently available.
